core_claim: Preference conflict resolution in group recommender systems (GRS) is the problem of producing a single ranked list for a set of users whose individual preferences disagree. The dominant approach borrows from social choice theory: compute individual predicted ratings, then aggregate them with an 'aggregation strategy' that encodes a normative stance on how conflict should be resolved. The three canonical families are: (1) Least Misery (LMS) — the group rating for an item is the MINIMUM of members' individual ratings, so the group is treated as only as happy as its unhappiest member (minimax / Rawlsian fairness); (2) Maximum/Average (Additive Utilitarian, ADD; or the mean) — the group rating is the SUM or MEAN of members' ratings, maximising aggregate utility; (3) Most Pleasure (MPL) — the group rating is the MAXIMUM of members' ratings, so the group is as happy as its happiest member. Other strategies sit between these (Average Without Misery = mean after dropping items any member rated below a threshold; Multiplicative; Fairness/turn-taking; Approval Voting; Majority; Borda count; Copeland). The theoretical link to viewing enjoyment: the choice of operator is not neutral — it determines WHOSE dissatisfaction the system is willing to tolerate. Least Misery embodies the intuition that a shared watch is ruined for everyone if one person hates the show (the 'veto' intuition), which is why it is the most-cited default for small co-present groups. Arrow's impossibility theorem implies no single strategy is optimal across all contexts, so the empirical question is which operator best predicts JOINT satisfaction under which group conditions — directly the HelpME2C two-person problem.
predictive_validity_for_media: Evidence is moderate and somewhat scenario-dependent; there is no universally winning strategy (this is the consistent meta-finding, traceable to Masthoff 2004 and reaffirmed by Arrow's theorem). Key empirical results: (1) Masthoff (2004, UMUAI 14:37-85) ran human-subjects studies in the TV domain where participants chose sequences for groups; participants spontaneously used computationally SIMPLE strategies — predominantly Average, Average Without Misery, and Least Misery — and switched between them by context. Multiplicative and Least Misery best matched human-chosen group ratings in some conditions; pure Most Pleasure was rarely used and judged poor. Participants explicitly cared about fairness, avoiding individual misery, and overall approval. (2) Barile, Najafian, Draws, Inel, Rieger, Hada & Tintarev (2021, PERSPECTIVES@RecSys, CEUR Vol-2955 paper11) ran a preregistered between-subjects study (N=399, Prolific) comparing five strategies (ADD, Approval Voting, LMS, Majority, MPL) on a 4-person restaurant scenario, measuring perceived fairness, consensus and satisfaction on a 7-point scale. Strong main effect of strategy on all three DVs (F = 36.19-49.57, all p<0.001, partial eta-squared 0.27-0.34 — large effects). Most Pleasure (MPL) scored DRAMATICALLY worst on fairness, consensus AND satisfaction (all posthoc p<0.001 vs every other strategy; means roughly -1.5 to -2 vs +0.5 to +1 for others). Majority and Approval Voting were judged LESS FAIR than Least Misery (p_adj = 0.005 and 0.004). Fairness and consensus perception correlated strongly with satisfaction (Spearman rho = 0.71 and 0.76, p<0.001). (3) Tran et al. (2019), using the SAME restaurant scenario, found MAJ and ADD scored BETTER than LMS — the opposite ordering — attributed to within-subjects vs between-subjects design differences; this is direct evidence that strategy ranking is fragile to experimental design and scenario. (4) Yu et al. (2006, UMUAI 16:63-82) built a group TV recommender merging individual feature-vector profiles by minimising total distance (a utilitarian-style aggregation rather than min/max); they reported the merged group profile produced acceptable group satisfaction but did not run a clean head-to-head of min vs mean vs max. Net: the construct predicts group satisfaction well enough to matter (large eta-squared), the robust directional finding is that MOST PLEASURE is reliably bad and LEAST MISERY is reliably perceived as FAIR; the average/utilitarian family is competitive and sometimes best. There is NO study isolating completion rate or thematic vs genre preference by aggregation strategy — those distinctions are unmeasured in this literature.
instrument_design: Not a psychometric instrument — these are aggregation OPERATORS applied to per-item, per-member predicted ratings, so 'instrument design' means the operator definitions and the inputs they require. Inputs: a matrix of individual ratings/predictions r(u,i) on a common scale (typically 1-5 or normalised 0-1). Operators (for group G, item i): Least Misery = min over u in G of r(u,i); Average = mean over u of r(u,i); Additive Utilitarian = sum over u of r(u,i) (equivalent ranking to Average for fixed |G|); Most Pleasure = max over u of r(u,i); Average Without Misery = mean of r(u,i) after removing items where any r(u,i) < threshold (threshold commonly 3 on a 1-5 scale); Multiplicative = product of r(u,i); Approval Voting = count of members with r(u,i) >= threshold; Borda/Copeland = rank-based social choice. There is no 'item count' or 'completion time' — the operator is O(|G|) per item, instantaneous. The minimum viable version for HelpME2C: a two-person operator over each candidate title's two predicted affinity scores. For the under-2-minute onboarding constraint, the instrument that FEEDS these operators (the per-user preference signal) is what must be short; the aggregation itself adds no user-facing friction. A defensible MVP: compute each user's predicted affinity to a candidate, then apply Least Misery as the primary ranker with Average as a tiebreaker — zero additional questions required.
cold_start_applicability: High applicability in principle, but ENTIRELY dependent on the quality of the individual preference signal it consumes. The aggregation operator itself is content-agnostic and works the instant two per-user scores exist — it adds no cold-start cost. The cold-start problem lives upstream: with zero viewing history, the individual r(u,i) predictions must come from a cold-start source (personality-to-genre mapping, declared favourites, theme elicitation). The aggregation layer is therefore the CHEAPEST part of the two-person pipeline to ship and the part LEAST sensitive to cold start — it is pure post-processing. Important caveat for cold start specifically: when individual predictions are noisy (as they always are at cold start), Least Misery AMPLIFIES noise — a single spuriously low predicted score vetoes an otherwise-good joint pick. Average is more robust to per-user prediction noise because errors partially cancel. This argues for Average (or Average-Without-Misery) as the cold-start-phase default and a shift toward Least Misery as per-user confidence rises with interaction data. For cross-medium inference, the operator is medium-agnostic: if a common affinity scale spans TV and anime, the same min/mean/max applies across mediums without modification.
group_compatibility_use: This IS the group-compatibility literature, and the two-person (dyad) case is the cleanest application — though, importantly, almost no study isolates n=2; samples use 3-8 members and aggregate. What transfers to the HelpME2C dyad: (1) Combination is meaningful and well-defined: two per-title affinity scores combine via min (Least Misery), mean (Average), or max (Most Pleasure). (2) Strongest empirical signal for the dyad use case is NEGATIVE — Most Pleasure (max) reliably destroys perceived fairness, consensus and satisfaction (Barile et al. 2021, large effect). For two people choosing one show, optimising for whoever is most excited is the worst option. (3) Least Misery is the most consistently FAIR-PERCEIVED operator and matches the strong dyadic intuition that a co-watch fails if either person hates it — the 'veto' logic is most legitimate at n=2 because there is no majority to outvote a dissenter. (4) BUT the average/utilitarian family sometimes beats Least Misery on satisfaction (Tran et al. 2019) and is more robust to noisy cold-start predictions. (5) Personality-aware aggregation (Najafian et al. PEGA, arXiv:2304.08851) argues fixed min/mean/max are too inflexible and that member influence should be WEIGHTED by personality — e.g. a more conformist/agreeable member yields, a 'stubborn' member gets up-weighted; ablation showed personality contributed ~6.4% N@20 vs ~2.5% for preference refinement, i.e. personality weighting carried 2-3x more signal than preference tuning. Quijano-Sanchez et al. additionally fold personality and tie-strength into the aggregation. For HelpME2C's ghost-profile / personality angle this is the most directly relevant frontier: rather than picking one operator, weight the two members' contributions by an inferred conformity/dominance trait, then aggregate. Recommended dyad strategy: Least Misery as the fairness backbone, Average as tiebreaker, with personality-derived weights modulating each member's contribution — but the weighting must be validated, as no study isolates the n=2 personality-weighted case.
quiz_ux_implications: The aggregation operator is invisible to the user — it requires no quiz items of its own, so its UX cost is zero and it introduces none of the response-bias problems of a personality instrument. The UX implications are downstream and twofold. (1) EXPLANATION: Barile et al. (2021) found, surprisingly, that natural-language EXPLANATIONS of the aggregation strategy had NO significant effect on perceived fairness, consensus, or satisfaction (F = 0.14-0.35, p > 0.71, eta-squared ~0.00) — perceptions were driven almost entirely by the strategy itself, not how it was explained. Implication: invest in choosing a good operator, not in elaborate explanation copy; a simple statement ('we picked something you'll both enjoy / something neither of you will dislike') suffices. (2) The veto/fairness framing matters: because Least Misery is perceived as fairest and Most Pleasure as least fair, the product should frame joint recommendations around 'something you'll both like' (Least-Misery framing) rather than 'the most exciting pick' (Most-Pleasure framing). No acquiescence/social-desirability/faking bias applies to the operator itself; those biases live in whatever upstream instrument elicits individual preferences. The only elicitation question the aggregation layer might motivate is an optional dominance/yielding signal ('when you and a friend can't agree, do you usually give in or hold out?') to feed personality-weighted aggregation — and that single item IS subject to social-desirability bias and should be gamified or inferred behaviourally rather than asked directly.
cross_cultural_validity: Weakly studied and a genuine gap. The aggregation operators are mathematical and culture-neutral in form, but the NORMATIVE assumption each encodes is culturally loaded. Least Misery (harm-avoidance, protect the weakest) vs Most Pleasure (maximise the best-off) vs Average (utilitarian) map onto documented cross-cultural differences in fairness norms (egalitarian vs equity vs need-based distribution) and individualism-collectivism. The foundational studies (Masthoff 2004 UK; Barile et al. 2021 Prolific English-speakers; Tran et al.; Yu et al.) are overwhelmingly Western and English-language; none reports a cross-cultural replication of which operator maximises perceived fairness. [uncertain] It is plausible — but not empirically established in this literature — that more collectivist cultures favour misery-avoidance/consensus operators while more individualist contexts tolerate utilitarian averaging; this should be treated as a hypothesis, not a finding. For HelpME2C's multilingual catalog the operator math is safe to reuse across locales, but the FRAMING/explanation copy and any default-strategy choice should not be assumed to carry equal fairness perception across cultures without local validation. The personality-weighting extension inherits whatever cross-cultural fragility the underlying personality instrument has (Big Five conformity/dominance constructs have documented non-Western measurement issues).
privacy_consent_implications: The aggregation operator over two users' predicted affinities does not itself collect new special-category data — it processes preference predictions the system already holds. The privacy-significant surface is (a) cross-user disclosure and (b) any personality input to weighted aggregation. (a) A naive explanation that reveals one member's individual ratings to the other ('Alex rated this 1 star') leaks personal preference data between users and can cause social friction; Barile et al. note privacy-preserving explanation as an explicit GRS goal (avoid disclosing individual preference info). Group explanations should aggregate, never expose, individual member scores by default — consistent with HelpME2C ADR-0012 and the GDPR data-minimisation posture. (b) If aggregation is WEIGHTED by an inferred personality/conformity trait, that inference can constitute GDPR Article 9 special-category data (personality profiling can be inferred to relate to health, beliefs, political opinion) and requires the same explicit-consent and minimisation treatment as any personality instrument — see the personality-instrument research items. The pure (unweighted) Least-Misery/Average operator carries LOW marginal privacy risk; the personality-weighted variant carries the personality instrument's full Article 9 risk. Recommendation: ship the unweighted operator first (low consent burden); gate personality-weighting behind explicit consent if/when introduced.
relationship_to_theme_vocabulary: Aggregation strategy is orthogonal to the theme vocabulary — it operates AFTER per-title, per-user affinity (including theme affinity) has been computed, so it does not predict preference for specific slugs. Instead it determines how two members' per-theme affinities are reconciled. The mapping that matters is between the CONFLICT PROFILE of a dyad and the right operator, expressed through themes: (1) When the two members have HIGH theme overlap (both score highly on, e.g., found-family, slow-burn, character-study), Average and Least Misery converge and either works — the operator choice is low-stakes. (2) When members DIVERGE sharply on intense/polarising themes (existential-horror, cosmic-horror, folk-horror, vengeance-and-its-cost, toxic-relationship, addiction-and-recovery, mental-illness-portrayal, war-and-its-toll) — themes where one member's strong aversion genuinely ruins a co-watch — Least Misery is the right operator because the veto intuition is legitimate: a title heavy in existential-horror should be down-ranked for the pair if EITHER member scores low on it. (3) For lighter, lower-variance themes (absurdist-comedy, heist-and-caper, fish-out-of-water, workplace-procedural, underdog-rise, whodunit) the cost of a mismatch is low, so Average is appropriate — no need to let one member veto a breezy pick. A practical design: apply Least Misery on a watchlist of 'aversion-sensitive' themes (the intense/dark/horror/trauma cluster) and Average on 'low-stakes' themes, then combine. This per-theme operator selection is a HelpME2C-specific synthesis, not drawn from a single source. [uncertain]
implementation_recommendation: INCLUDE — the aggregation layer is the load-bearing component of the two-person recommendation and is cheap, invisible, and well-supported. Concrete recommendation: (1) Ship Least Misery as the PRIMARY joint ranker, with Average as a tiebreaker (LMS-then-mean). Rationale: across the cleanest preregistered evidence (Barile et al. 2021, large effect sizes) Least Misery is the most consistently FAIR-perceived and Most Pleasure is reliably the worst — EXCLUDE Most Pleasure entirely as a default. The dyadic veto intuition makes Least Misery the most legitimate operator at n=2. (2) For the COLD-START phase specifically, lean on Average (or Average-Without-Misery, threshold ~3/5) rather than pure Least Misery, because noisy per-user predictions cause Least Misery to over-veto; transition toward Least Misery as per-user confidence grows. This is a confidence-gated blend, not a fixed choice. (3) Operator selection adds ZERO onboarding items — do not add quiz questions for it. (4) Do NOT invest in elaborate strategy explanations (Barile et al.: explanations had null effect); a one-line 'something you'll both enjoy' framing suffices, and explanations must aggregate not expose individual scores. (5) DEFER personality-weighted aggregation (PEGA / Quijano-Sanchez style) to a later phase — it is the most promising lever for the personality/ghost-profile differentiator and showed strong ablation signal, but it is unvalidated at n=2, inherits Article 9 consent burden, and depends on a validated conformity/dominance instrument. Flag dependency: personality-weighting is 'only useful if combined with a validated personality/values elicitation' (see personality-instrument research items). What would change the cold-start default away from Average: a HelpME2C-internal A/B test on joint-watch completion showing Least Misery wins even at low confidence.
key_sources: 1. Masthoff, J. (2004) 'Group Modeling: Selecting a Sequence of Television Items to Suit a Group of Viewers', UMUAI 14:37-85 — foundational; humans use Average / Average-Without-Misery / Least Misery, no dominant strategy (paywalled; widely summarised, free preprints of related chapter at https://www.researchgate.net/publication/227132202). 2. Barile, Najafian, Draws, Inel, Rieger, Hada, Tintarev (2021) 'Toward Benchmarking Group Explanations: Evaluating the Effect of Aggregation Strategies versus Explanation', PERSPECTIVES@RecSys, CEUR Vol-2955 paper11 — preregistered N=399, large strategy effects, Most Pleasure worst, Least Misery fairest (FREE: https://ceur-ws.org/Vol-2955/paper11.pdf; data https://osf.io/5xbgf/). 3. Masthoff, J. (2015/2022) 'Group Recommender Systems: Aggregation, Satisfaction and Group Attributes', in Recommender Systems Handbook (paywalled: https://link.springer.com/chapter/10.1007/978-1-4899-7637-6_22). 4. Najafian et al. 'A Personality-Guided Preference Aggregator for Ephemeral Group Recommendation' (PEGA), arXiv:2304.08851 — personality-weighted aggregation, ablation 6.4% (FREE: https://arxiv.org/html/2304.08851v2). 5. Yu, Zhou, Hao, Gu (2006) 'TV Program Recommendation for Multiple Viewers Based on User Profile Merging', UMUAI 16:63-82 — group TV via profile-distance merging (PDF host had cert issue; via Springer https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11257-006-9008-3). 6. Masthoff (2006) 'In pursuit of satisfaction and the prevention of embarrassment: affective state in group recommender systems', UMUAI 16 — emotional contagion among group members (paywalled: https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11257-006-9008-3). 7. 'An overview of consensus models for group decision-making and GRS' UMUAI 2023 (https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11257-023-09380-z). 8. Tran et al. (2019) explanation/strategy study using the restaurant scenario — cited in source 2 as the within-subjects comparison where MAJ/ADD beat LMS.
